mappings.rationale: Directly covers subsection C on the Mongol Empire and its successor states, including Genghis Khan's conquests, the division among his sons, and the Timurid dynasty.
Directly relevant to the section's coverage of Inner Asian trade routes, the development of West and East Turkistan, and the connections between Central Asian states and their neighbours.
Directly relevant to subsection D on Tibet, including the introduction of Buddhism, the Yellow Hat monastic order, and Tibet's religious and cultural development.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the revival of Buddhism in Mongolia and its introduction and resurgence in Tibet.
Relevant to subsection D.2 on Nepal's rule by Indian princely families and the influence of Hinduism.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Muslim rule in Afghanistan, the conversion of the Golden Horde to Islam, and the Islamization of Turkic peoples.
The section explicitly discusses historiographic problems in studying Inner Asian history and the interpretation of archaeological and documentary sources.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of dynastic succession across Inner Asia, including the Mongol khanates, Timurids, and various Turkic ruling families.
Relevant to subsection C.2.d on the Golden Horde and the Kipchak empire in eastern Europe, and Russian expansion into Siberia.
